children.name: Pipistrellus
children.description: Pipistrelles — the smallest and most abundant bats in Europe. The common pipistrelle weighs just 3–8 grams but can consume 3,000 midges per night, making it an extraordinarily effective insect controller. Pipistrelles echolocate at characteristic frequencies that allow species identification: the common pipistrelle calls at around 45 kHz, the soprano pipistrelle at 55 kHz. These two species were only distinguished in the 1990s using bat detectors and now known to be genetically and ecologically distinct. They roost in crevices of buildings and trees, with maternity colonies forming in spring.

children.name: Pipistrellus pipistrellus
children.description: Britain's most common bat. Weighs as little as 3 g. A single individual may eat 3,000 insects in a single night. First bat to emerge after dusk, often visible over gardens and along woodland edges.

children.name: Pipistrellus pygmaeus
children.description: Not separated from the common pipistrelle until 1999. Calls at 55 kHz versus 45 kHz for common pipistrelle. Strongly associated with riparian habitats and tends to roost closer to water.

children.name: Plecotus
children.description: Long-eared bats — among the most distinctive of European bats, with ears nearly as long as their body that fold down under the wing membrane when roosting. The exceptionally large ears are an adaptation for detecting the faint rustling sounds of resting insects on leaves — they are 'gleaners' that can pluck moths and beetles off foliage as well as catching them in flight. Long-eared bats are among the most sedentary of European bats, rarely moving more than 10 km from their roost. They are particularly sensitive to loss of old buildings, which provide their preferred maternity roosts.

children.name: Plecotus auritus
children.description: The second most common bat in Britain. Roosts in the apex of old buildings and churches. The enormous ears give them exceptional directional hearing for detecting prey on leaves and bark.

children.name: Plecotus austriacus
children.description: Rare and declining across its European range. In Britain, fewer than 1,000 individuals survive. Has a more southerly and coastal distribution than the brown long-eared bat.

children.name: Nyctalus
children.description: Noctules — large, fast-flying bats that hunt in open airspace high above the canopy, taking large moths, cockchafers, and even other bats. The common noctule is one of the fastest bats in Europe, reaching 50 km/h and commuting up to 100 km to good foraging sites. Noctules roost almost exclusively in tree holes — old woodpecker cavities and natural splits — and are highly migratory across Europe, tracking insect abundance across seasons. Their calls (typically 20–25 kHz) can be detected easily with bat detectors and are one of the first nocturnal sounds heard after dusk.

children.name: Nyctalus noctula
children.description: Often the first bat of the evening, visible high above treelines before dusk. A long-distance migrant; Scandinavian noctules winter in southern Europe. Hunts moths and beetles detected at long range with low-frequency echolocation.

children.name: Myotis
children.description: The largest bat genus, with over 120 species worldwide. Myotis bats are diverse in their foraging strategies: Daubenton's bat skims the water surface catching emerging insects and even small fish; the whiskered bat hunts through woodland; the greater mouse-eared bat, one of the largest European bats, hunts large beetles on the ground. Most Myotis species roost in caves for winter hibernation, where they form mixed-species colonies. Whitenosesyndrome — caused by the fungus Pseudogymnoascus destructans — has killed millions of hibernating Myotis bats in North America since 2006.

children.name: Myotis daubentonii
children.description: The 'water bat' — a familiar sight skimming rivers and lakes just centimetres above the surface, scooping insects and occasionally small fish with its large hind feet. Roosts in bridges and tunnels near water.

children.name: Myotis mystacinus
children.description: One of the smallest Myotis species. Hunts in woodland edge, gardens, and along hedgerows. Forms maternity roosts in old buildings; one of the commonest bat species found roosting in Swedish churches.

children.name: Eptesicus
children.description: Serotines — large, slow-flying bats that hunt in open woodland and parkland, taking large moths and beetles. The serotine is one of the largest British bats, appearing similar to a noctule but with a distinctly longer, broader wing tip allowing slower, more manoeuvrable flight. They roost exclusively in buildings — often in the roofspace or within cavity walls — and rarely use caves. The big brown bat (Eptesicus fuscus) is the most widespread bat in North America and the species most frequently found roosting in houses there.

children.name: Eptesicus serotinus
children.description: One of the largest bats in Europe; wingspan up to 38 cm. Has a distinctive low, slow flight. Roosts only in buildings; a maternity roost in a house roof may persist for decades or centuries.
